cue: How do you tell someone to stop putting it off?

es.text: No dejes para mañana lo que puedas hacer hoy
es.literal: do not leave for tomorrow what you can do today
es.meaning: Don't postpone what you're able to do now.
es.example: Manda el correo antes de irte: no dejes para mañana lo que puedas hacer hoy.
es.exampleTranslation: Send the email before you leave — don't put off what you can do today.

it.text: Chi ha tempo non aspetti tempo
it.literal: whoever has time, let them not wait for time
it.meaning: If you have the chance now, don't wait for a better moment.
it.example: Firmalo adesso: chi ha tempo non aspetti tempo.
it.exampleTranslation: Sign it now — if you can do it today, don't wait.
it.regionNote: The everyday twin of the Spanish is 'non rimandare a domani quello che puoi fare oggi'. This older proverb is the more idiomatic Italian and is paired here deliberately; the literal twin is recorded as an alternative pairing.

bridge.note: Both proverbs attack delay, but they grab different things. Spanish moves the TASK: don't shift it into tomorrow. Italian moves the TIME: if you have it, don't sit waiting for more of it — and it says so by using the word twice, first meaning the time you already hold and then the better moment you're hoping for. Spanish scolds the procrastinator; Italian catches the specific self-deception that makes procrastination feel reasonable.

interference.es_to_it: The word-for-word Italian version exists and is understood, but this older proverb is the idiomatic one — and note the subjunctive: non ASPETTI, not 'non aspetta'.
interference.it_to_es: The doubled tempo has no Spanish equivalent; 'quien tiene tiempo no espere tiempo' is not a refrán. Spanish frames it as the task deferred.

review.notes: Prompted by Joe sharing the English aphorism 'life is not a rehearsal' 2026-07-18 (authored, no fixed counterpart, not bankable itself). IT side deliberately NOT the literal twin — that pairing is filed in alternatives.json as A005. Reviewer to confirm 'chi ha tempo non aspetti tempo' is still current speech rather than dictionary-only, and to sanity-check the subjunctive form.
